title: HTTP Representations, Validators, Ranges, and File Transfer · Runtime Contract: Stream Backpressure
summary: A download endpoint guesses MIME types, buffers large bodies, ignores validators and Range, and treats Content-Disposition text as trusted HTML. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the stream backpressure increment in HTTP file-representation and transfer contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Stream through bounded buffers with cancellation, backpressure, body ownership, partial-write awareness, and cleanup.
Parse and evaluate single byte ranges, suffix ranges, satisfiable bounds, 206, Content-Range, Accept-Ranges, and 416.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Stream Backpressure · Theory Stream Backpressure
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 4469 during a private media release. Build deterministic Go evidence to validate conditional inclusive byte ranges and response-body cleanup; do not treat an ETag, CORS result, emulator, or successful SDK call as stronger proof than it is; then change the object version and prove stale authority is rejected. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Stream through bounded buffers with cancellation, backpressure, body ownership, partial-write awareness, and cleanup.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Stream Backpressure contract for the supplied data.
